label: UI Mockup Brief
description: A precise brief to generate or design a screen.

fields.label: Screen / component
fields.placeholder: Billing settings page

fields.label: User goal on this screen
fields.placeholder: Let an admin view their plan, change it, and update the payment method.

fields.label: Style direction
fields.placeholder: Clean, modern SaaS; neutral palette; subtle depth

fields.label: Must-have elements
fields.placeholder: Current plan card, plan comparison, payment method, invoices table.

template:
You are a senior product designer. Produce a {{fidelity}} design brief for the "{{screen}}" screen.

## User goal
{{purpose}}

## Constraints
- Platform: {{platform}}.
{{#if style}}- Style direction: {{style}}.
{{/if}}{{#if elements}}- Must include: {{elements}}.
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- Describe layout as a clear hierarchy (regions, sections, components) with intent for each.
- Specify spacing rhythm, type roles, and color usage at a system level (not pixel-by-pixel unless production fidelity).
- Define the primary action and how the layout guides the user to it.
{{#if states}}- Cover empty, loading, error, and success states explicitly.
{{/if}}- Note accessibility: contrast, focus order, touch targets, and labels.

## Deliverables
1. A structured layout description an AI image/UI tool can render faithfully.
2. A short rationale for the two most important decisions.
{{#if fidelity == "production"}}3. Component-level specs ready for a developer to implement.
{{/if}}

label: Design System

fields.label: Product
fields.placeholder: A developer-tools dashboard

fields.label: Brand personality
fields.placeholder: Precise, trustworthy, a little playful. Dark-mode first.

template:
You are a design-systems lead. Define a design system.

## Product
{{product}}

## Brand personality
{{brand}}

## Scope
- Platforms: {{platform}}.
- Cover: {{scope}}.

## Requirements
- Start from principles, then derive tokens — explain the reasoning, don't just list values.
{{#if scope == "color"}}- Color: semantic roles (bg, surface, text, primary, danger…), light/dark, all pairs AA-contrast.
{{/if}}{{#if scope == "type"}}- Typography: a modular scale with roles and line-heights.
{{/if}}{{#if scope == "space"}}- Spacing: a consistent scale and layout/grid rules.
{{/if}}{{#if scope == "components"}}- Components: states (default/hover/focus/active/disabled) and usage rules for buttons, inputs, cards.
{{/if}}{{#if scope == "motion"}}- Motion: duration/easing tokens and when to use them.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The token set with rationale.
{{#if framework == "tailwind"}}2. A ready-to-use Tailwind theme config.
{{/if}}{{#if framework == "css"}}2. A `:root` CSS custom-property sheet (incl. dark mode).
{{/if}}3. Do/don't examples for the trickiest decisions.

label: User Flow
description: An end-to-end flow an AI/team can build from.

fields.label: Flow name
fields.placeholder: New user onboarding

fields.label: Primary actor
fields.placeholder: A first-time admin invited by a teammate

fields.label: Their goal
fields.placeholder: Get from invite email to a configured workspace with data connected.

template:
You are a senior UX designer. Map the "{{flowName}}" user flow.

## Actor
{{actor}}

## Goal
{{goal}}

## Platform
{{platform}}

## Requirements
- Describe the flow as ordered steps: for each, the screen, what the user sees, the action, and the system response.
- Note the decision points and what determines each branch.
- Keep the path to the goal as short as honestly possible; flag friction.
{{#if edgeCases}}- Map the important error/edge paths (invalid input, no data, permission denied, drop-off) and the recovery.
{{/if}}{{#if metrics}}- Define success metrics and the key drop-off points to instrument.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The step-by-step flow (numbered, with branches).
2. A compact text flow diagram (Step → Step, branches labeled).
3. The top 3 design risks and how to de-risk them.

label: Wireframe Brief
description: Low-fi layout spec focused on structure, not visuals.

fields.label: Screen / page
fields.placeholder: Project dashboard

fields.label: What the user is here to do
fields.placeholder: Quickly see project health and jump into the one that needs attention.

fields.label: Content & elements to place
fields.placeholder: KPI row, project list, filters, recent activity, primary 'New project' action.

fields.label: Information priority order
fields.placeholder: Attention items > active projects > metrics > activity

label: Design Critique
description: Structured, actionable critique of an existing design.

fields.label: What is being reviewed
fields.placeholder: Checkout flow, final hi-fi mockups

fields.label: Stated design goal
fields.placeholder: Reduce checkout abandonment by simplifying to two steps.

fields.label: Design description / what you see
fields.placeholder: Describe the screens, layout, flow, and notable choices to critique.

template:
You are a design lead running a rigorous critique of: {{artifact}}.

## Stated goal
{{designIntent}}

## Design under review
{{designDetails}}

## Lenses
Critique through: {{lens}}.

## Audience & tone
- End user: {{audience}}.
- Tone: {{tone}} — specific, kind, and direct.

## Requirements
- Judge against the stated goal first, then craft and best practice.
- For each issue: what, why it matters (user impact), and a concrete suggested fix.
- Call out what works well and why, so it is preserved.
{{#if lens == "a11y"}}- Flag contrast, focus order, target size, and labeling problems explicitly.
{{/if}}{{#if prioritize}}- Group findings into Must-fix / Should-fix / Polish by user impact.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The critique organized by lens (or by priority).
2. The single highest-leverage change to make first.
3. 3 open questions for the designer.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Usability Test Plan
description: A research plan to validate a design with real users.

fields.label: Product / feature under test
fields.placeholder: New onboarding wizard

fields.label: Hypotheses / concerns
fields.placeholder: Users won't understand the workspace vs. project distinction in step 2.

fields.label: Target participants
fields.placeholder: 5-7 admins new to the product, mix of technical and non-technical.

fields.label: Candidate tasks
fields.placeholder: Create a workspace, invite a teammate, connect a data source.

template:
You are a senior UX researcher. Write a usability test plan for: {{product}}.

## Hypotheses / concerns to test
{{hypotheses}}

## Participants
{{participants}}

## Method
{{method}} sessions.
{{#if tasks}}
## Candidate tasks
{{tasks}}
{{/if}}
## Stakeholders
Results will be reported to: {{audience}}.

## Requirements
- Tie every task to a hypothesis; tasks must be realistic and scenario-framed, not leading.
- Provide a moderator script: intro, warm-up, tasks, probes, wrap-up.
- Define pass/fail criteria per task and what would change the design.
{{#if metrics}}- Specify metrics: task success, time on task, error count, and a post-task ease rating.
{{/if}}{{#if method == "unmoderated"}}- Adapt instructions to be fully self-guided with no facilitator present.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The full test plan (objectives, participants, tasks, script, metrics).
2. A screening questionnaire.
3. A results-readout template aligned to the hypotheses.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Accessibility Review
description: Audit a design or UI against WCAG and inclusive practice.

fields.label: What is being audited
fields.placeholder: Settings page, hi-fi mockups + prototype

fields.label: UI description / components
fields.placeholder: Describe layout, colors, interactive elements, and any known issues.

template:
You are an accessibility specialist. Audit {{target}} against WCAG.

## UI under review
{{description}}

## Standard
Target conformance: {{level}} on {{platform}}.

## Focus areas
{{focus}}

## Requirements
- Map each finding to a specific WCAG success criterion (number + name) and a severity.
- Be concrete about who is blocked and how (e.g., keyboard-only, low vision, screen reader).
{{#if focus == "contrast"}}- Check text and non-text contrast ratios against the target level.
{{/if}}{{#if focus == "keyboard"}}- Verify focus order, visible focus, and that nothing is keyboard-trapped.
{{/if}}{{#if focus == "forms"}}- Check label association, error identification, and instructions.
{{/if}}{{#if remediation}}- For every issue give a concrete remediation a designer/developer can apply.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. A findings table: criterion, issue, severity, recommendation.
2. The top 3 blocking issues to fix first.
3. A short note on what already conforms well.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: UX Microcopy
description: Interface text: labels, buttons, errors and helper text.

fields.label: Where this copy lives
fields.placeholder: Delete-account confirmation modal

fields.label: User situation & emotion
fields.placeholder: User is irreversibly deleting their account; likely anxious, needs reassurance + a clear out.

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Button labels <= 2 words; no jargon; localizable

template:
You are a senior UX writer. Write microcopy for: {{context}}.

## User situation
{{situation}}

## Elements to write
{{elements}}

## Voice & audience
- Voice/tone: {{voice}}.
- Audience: {{audience}}.
{{#if constraints}}- Constraints: {{constraints}}.
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- Lead with the user's need; be concrete, calm, and free of jargon and dark patterns.
{{#if elements == "buttons"}}- Buttons: action-led verbs; the primary action says exactly what happens.
{{/if}}{{#if elements == "errors"}}- Errors: say what went wrong, why, and the next step — never blame the user.
{{/if}}{{#if elements == "empty"}}- Empty/success: orient the user and offer a clear next action.
{{/if}}{{#if variants}}- Provide 2 alternates per element with a one-line note on the trade-off.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The copy, organized by element.
2. A one-line voice rationale.
3. Any element where the UX itself (not the words) is the real problem.

Deliver final, paste-ready copy; assume sensible defaults.

label: Empty & Edge States
description: Design the zero, loading, error and edge states of a UI.

fields.label: Feature / screen
fields.placeholder: Projects list

fields.label: What it looks like with data
fields.placeholder: A grid of project cards with filters and a primary 'New project' button.

template:
You are a product designer specializing in resilient UI states. Design the states for: {{feature}}.

## Happy path (for contrast)
{{happyPath}}

## States to design
{{states}}

## User & tone
- User: {{audience}}.
- Tone: {{tone}}.

## Requirements
- For each state define: what the user sees, the message, the primary action, and the visual treatment.
- Every state must give the user a way forward — no dead ends.
{{#if states == "firstuse"}}- First use: teach the value and offer a one-click way to get the first result (consider sample data).
{{/if}}{{#if states == "loading"}}- Loading: prefer skeletons that match the real layout; avoid layout shift.
{{/if}}{{#if states == "error"}}- Error: explain plainly, offer a retry, and never lose the user's context/work.
{{/if}}{{#if includeCopy}}- Provide the exact microcopy (heading, body, button) for each state.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. A spec per state (visual + behavior + copy).
2. A decision table mapping conditions to which state shows.
3. The state most often skipped by teams and why it matters here.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Design-to-Dev Handoff
description: A complete spec so engineers can build with no guesswork.

fields.label: Feature / component
fields.placeholder: Multi-step invite flow

fields.label: Design summary & screens
fields.placeholder: Describe the screens, layout, and key interactions being handed off.

fields.label: Interactions & behavior
fields.placeholder: Validation timing, optimistic updates, what happens on submit/cancel/back.

template:
You are a senior product designer writing an engineering handoff for: {{feature}}.

## Design summary
{{designSummary}}

## Interactions & behavior
{{behavior}}

## Target
Building for: {{platform}}. Receiving team: {{audience}}.

## Include
{{include}}

## Requirements
- Be unambiguous: every interaction has a defined trigger, result, and failure path.
- Reference tokens/values by name, not hardcoded magic numbers, where possible.
{{#if include == "states"}}- Spec every component state: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error.
{{/if}}{{#if include == "responsive"}}- Define behavior at each breakpoint and what reflows/collapses.
{{/if}}{{#if include == "a11y"}}- Include roles, labels, focus order, and keyboard interactions.
{{/if}}{{#if acceptance}}- Provide testable acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then form.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The implementation spec organized by section.
2. An open-questions list with your recommended default for each.
3. The single most likely thing to be built wrong, called out up top.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.
